学生手册2025
德才兼备
全面发展
求是创新
追求卓越
普通高等学校学生管理规定
中华人民共和国教育部令第41 号
第一章
总
则
第一条
为规范普通高等学校学生管理行为袁维护普通高等
学校正常的教育教学秩序和生活秩序袁保障学生合法权益袁培养
德尧智尧体尧美等方面全面发展的社会主义建设者和接班人,依据
教育法尧高等教育法以及有关法律尧法规袁制定本规定遥
第二条
本规定适用于普通高等学校尧承担研究生教育任务
的科学研究机构渊以下称学校冤对接受普通高等学历教育的研究
生和本科尧专科渊高职冤学生渊以下称学生冤的管理遥
第三条
学校要坚持社会主义办学方向袁坚持马克思主义的
指导地位,全面贯彻国家教育方针曰要坚持以立德树人为根本袁以
理想信念教育为核心,培育和践行社会主义核心价值观袁弘扬中华
优秀传统文化和革命文化尧社会主义先进文化袁培养学生的社会
责任感尧创新精神和实践能力曰要坚持依法治校袁科学管理袁健
全和完善管理制度袁规范管理行为袁将管理与育人相结合袁不断
提高管理和服务水平遥
第四条
学生应当拥护中国共产党领导袁努力学习马克思列
宁主义尧毛泽东思想尧中国特色社会主义理论体系袁深入学习习
近平总书记系列重要讲话精神和治国理政新理念新思想新战略袁
坚定中国特色社会主义道路自信尧理论自信尧制度自信尧文化自
信袁树立中国特色社会主义共同理想曰应当树立爱国主义思想袁
具有团结统一尧爱好和平尧勤劳勇敢尧自强不息的精神曰应当增
1

＞＞＞＞＞
Zhejiang University
学生手册2025
强法治观念袁遵守宪法尧法律尧法规袁遵守公民道德规范袁遵守
学校管理制度袁具有良好的道德品质和行为习惯曰应当刻苦学习袁
勇于探索袁积极实践袁努力掌握现代科学文化知识和专业技能曰
应当积极锻炼身体袁增进身心健康袁提高个人修养袁培养审美情
趣遥
第五条
实施学生管理袁应当尊重和保护学生的合法权利袁
教育和引导学生承担应尽的义务与责任袁鼓励和支持学生实行自
我管理尧自我服务尧自我教育尧自我监督遥
第二章
学生的权利与义务
第六条
学生在校期间依法享有下列权利院
渊一冤参加学校教育教学计划安排的各项活动袁使用学校提供
的教育教学资源曰
渊二冤参加社会实践尧志愿服务尧勤工助学尧文娱体育及科技
文化创新等活动袁获得就业创业指导和服务曰
渊三冤申请奖学金尧助学金及助学贷款曰
渊四冤在思想品德尧学业成绩等方面获得科学尧公正评价袁完
成学校规定学业后获得相应的学历证书尧学位证书曰
渊五冤在校内组织尧参加学生团体袁以适当方式参与学校管
理袁对学校与学生权益相关事务享有知情权尧参与权尧表达权和
监督权曰
渊六冤对学校给予的处理或者处分有异议袁向学校尧教育行政
部门提出申诉袁对学校尧教职员工侵犯其人身权尧财产权等合法
权益的行为袁提出申诉或者依法提起诉讼曰
渊七冤法律尧法规及学校章程规定的其他权利遥
第七条
学生在校期间依法履行下列义务院
渊一冤遵守宪法和法律尧法规曰
2

学生手册2025
德才兼备
全面发展
求是创新
追求卓越
渊二冤遵守学校章程和规章制度曰
渊三冤恪守学术道德袁完成规定学业曰
渊四冤按规定缴纳学费及有关费用袁履行获得贷学金及助学金
的相应义务曰
渊五冤遵守学生行为规范袁尊敬师长袁养成良好的思想品德和
行为习惯曰
渊六冤法律尧法规及学校章程规定的其他义务遥
第三章
学籍管理
第一节
入学与注册
第八条
按国家招生规定录取的新生袁持录取通知书袁按学
校有关要求和规定的期限到校办理入学手续遥因故不能按期入学
的袁应当向学校请假遥未请假或者请假逾期的袁除因不可抗力等
正当事由以外袁视为放弃入学资格遥
第九条
学校应当在报到时对新生入学资格进行初步审查袁
审查合格的办理入学手续袁予以注册学籍曰审查发现新生的录取
通知尧考生信息等证明材料袁与本人实际情况不符袁或者有其他
违反国家招生考试规定情形的袁取消入学资格遥
第十条
新生可以申请保留入学资格遥保留入学资格期间不
具有学籍遥保留入学资格的条件尧期限等由学校规定遥
新生保留入学资格期满前应向学校申请入学袁经学校审查合
格后袁办理入学手续遥审查不合格的袁取消入学资格曰逾期不办
理入学手续且未有因不可抗力延迟等正当理由的袁视为放弃入学
资格遥
第十一条
学生入学后袁学校应当在3 个月内按照国家招生
规定进行复查遥复查内容主要包括以下方面院
3

＞＞＞＞＞
Zhejiang University
学生手册2025
渊一冤录取手续及程序等是否合乎国家招生规定曰
渊二冤所获得的录取资格是否真实尧合乎相关规定曰
渊三冤本人及身份证明与录取通知尧考生档案等是否一致曰
渊四冤身心健康状况是否符合报考专业或者专业类别体检要
求袁能否保证在校正常学习尧生活曰
渊五冤艺术尧体育等特殊类型录取学生的专业水平是否符合录
取要求遥
复查中发现学生存在弄虚作假尧徇私舞弊等情形的袁确定为
复查不合格袁应当取消学籍曰情节严重的袁学校应当移交有关部
门调查处理遥
复查中发现学生身心状况不适宜在校学习袁经学校指定的二
级甲等以上医院诊断袁需要在家休养的袁可以按照第十条的规定
保留入学资格遥
复查的程序和办法袁由学校规定遥
第十二条
每学期开学时袁学生应当按学校规定办理注册手
续遥不能如期注册的袁应当履行暂缓注册手续遥未按学校规定缴
纳学费或者有其他不符合注册条件的袁不予注册遥
家庭经济困难的学生可以申请助学贷款或者其他形式资助袁
办理有关手续后注册遥
学校应当按照国家有关规定为家庭经济困难学生提供教育救
助袁完善学生资助体系袁保证学生不因家庭经济困难而放弃学业遥
第二节
考核与成绩记载
第十三条
学生应当参加学校教育教学计划规定的课程和各
种教育教学环节渊以下统称课程冤的考核袁考核成绩记入成绩册袁
并归入学籍档案遥
考核分为考试和考查两种遥考核和成绩评定方式袁以及考核
不合格的课程是否重修或者补考袁由学校规定遥
4

学生手册2025
德才兼备
全面发展
求是创新
追求卓越
第十四条
学生思想品德的考核尧鉴定袁以本规定第四条为
主要依据袁采取个人小结尧师生民主评议等形式进行遥
学生体育成绩评定要突出过程管理袁可以根据考勤尧课内教
学尧课外锻炼活动和体质健康等情况综合评定遥
第十五条
学生每学期或者每学年所修课程或者应修学分数
以及升级尧跳级尧留级尧降级等要求袁由学校规定遥
第十六条
学生根据学校有关规定袁可以申请辅修校内其他
专业或者选修其他专业课程曰可以申请跨校辅修专业或者修读课
程袁参加学校认可的开放式网络课程学习遥学生修读的课程成绩
渊学分冤袁学校审核同意后袁予以承认遥
第十七条
学生参加创新创业尧社会实践等活动以及发表论
文尧获得专利授权等与专业学习尧学业要求相关的经历尧成果袁
可以折算为学分袁计入学业成绩遥具体办法由学校规定遥
学校应当鼓励尧支持和指导学生参加社会实践尧创新创业活
动袁可以建立创新创业档案尧设置创新创业学分遥
第十八条
学校应当健全学生学业成绩和学籍档案管理制度袁
真实尧完整地记载尧出具学生学业成绩袁对通过补考尧重修获得
的成绩袁应当予以标注遥
学生严重违反考核纪律或者作弊的袁该课程考核成绩记为无
效袁并应视其违纪或者作弊情节袁给予相应的纪律处分遥给予警
告尧严重警告尧记过及留校察看处分的袁经教育表现较好袁可以
对该课程给予补考或者重修机会遥
学生因退学等情况中止学业袁其在校学习期间所修课程及已
获得学分袁应当予以记录遥学生重新参加入学考试尧符合录取条
件袁再次入学的袁其已获得学分袁经录取学校认定袁可以予以承
认遥具体办法由学校规定遥
第十九条
学生应当按时参加教育教学计划规定的活动遥不
能按时参加的袁应当事先请假并获得批准遥无故缺席的袁根据学
5

＞＞＞＞＞
Zhejiang University
学生手册2025
校有关规定给予批评教育袁情节严重的袁给予相应的纪律处分遥
第二十条
学校应当开展学生诚信教育袁以适当方式记录学
生学业尧学术尧品行等方面的诚信信息袁建立对失信行为的约束
和惩戒机制曰对有严重失信行为的袁可以规定给予相应的纪律处
分袁对违背学术诚信的袁可以对其获得学位及学术称号尧荣誉等
作出限制遥
第三节
转专业与转学
第二十一条
学生在学习期间对其他专业有兴趣和专长的袁
可以申请转专业曰以特殊招生形式录取的学生袁国家有相关规定
或者录取前与学校有明确约定的袁不得转专业遥
学校应当制定学生转专业的具体办法袁建立公平尧公正的标
准和程序袁健全公示制度遥学校根据社会对人才需求情况的发展
变化袁需要适当调整专业的袁应当允许在读学生转到其他相关专
业就读遥
休学创业或退役后复学的学生袁因自身情况需要转专业的袁
学校应当优先考虑遥
第二十二条
学生一般应当在被录取学校完成学业遥因患病
或者有特殊困难尧特别需要袁无法继续在本校学习或者不适应本
校学习要求的袁可以申请转学遥有下列情形之一袁不得转学院
渊一冤入学未满一学期或者毕业前一年的曰
渊二冤高考成绩低于拟转入学校相关专业同一生源地相应年份
录取成绩的曰

1. Program Educational Objective 
This program is an ideal choice for those who wish to pursue studies in Industrial Automation, 
System Control, and Robotics. It addresses latest technologies and their applications in control 
science and engineering, enabling students to explore new technology innovations to serve 
social demands.

1) 
Curriculum Requirement 
The curriculum leading to the degree of Bachelor of Science in Automation from ZJU requires 
135 hours and is organized into required courses, technical electives, liberal education, 
Chinese language and society study and other electives.

Course Drop Request
Before you make a decision to drop a course, please understand that you are not
encouraged to drop any required courses that will affect your progress towards
graduation. In addition, you should follow the instructions below to drop a course
that you enrolled already.
ZJUI Academic Affairs
1. During the first week of the semester, you can drop a course by yourself
through PeopleSoft.
2. If later than the first week, you must submit a request here by the end of 7th
week of instruction in a semester. For Fall 2025, the deadline of drop course is
5PM, Nov 7, 2025. There will be no W (Withdraw) recording on your
transcript if your request is approved. If the course follows UIUC calendar (the
course starts on August 25), the deadline is Oct 17 2025 (U

equest is approved. If the course follows UIUC calendar (the
course starts on August 25), the deadline is Oct 17 2025 (UIUC time).
3. If the course you want to drop is UIUC credit (click here to check, UIUC credit
courses are highlighted in yellow), you must submit an additional request
through UIUC COE portal with your UUC NetID by the above deadline. For
the students who pursue ZJU single degree, you do not need to submit
request to UIUC portal.
4. Please don't submit your request on last minute. You will be not be allowed
to drop course after the deadline if nothing happens beyond your control.
5. For some 100- and 200-level courses, you need to go through the portal to
drop the course if you accidently add the course and cannot drop it by self-
service in PS system.

Course Offering in FA 2025
All the courses offered in FA 2025 are categorized into the following three types: 
 
Courses are also categorized as mandatory (section 3 in your curriculum) and
elective ones (section 4 in your curriculum). 
 
Please read all the following notes before dive into the course table.
1. UIUC courses are those taught by UIUC faculties or co-taught by ZJUI and
UIUC faculties. The academic credits (AU) earned from UIUC courses are
therefore counted as UIUC credits. Final grades will be recorded in both ZJUI
and UIUC systems.
All UIUC courses are highlighted in blue in the course tables.
2. ZJUI courses are those taught by ZJUI faculties normally without the
involvement of UIUC faculties. AUs earned from ZJUI courses are therefore
counted as ZJUI credits.

normally without the

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Civil engineering is a profession that applies the basic principles of science in conjunction with 
mathematical and computational tools to solve problems associated with developing and 
sustaining civilized life on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and the

ife on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and they usually require cooperation among 
professionals of many different disciplines. The completion of a civil engineering project 
involves the solution of technical problems in which uncertainty of information and myriad non-
technical factors often play a significant role. Some of the most common examples of civil 
engineering works include bridges, buildings, dams, airports, highways, tunnels, and water 
distribution systems. Civil engineers are concerned with flood control, landslides, air and water 
pollution, and the design of facilities to withstand earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil

earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil Engineering program comprises seven main areas (construction engineering and 
management, construction materials engineering, environmental engineering, geotechnical 
engineering, environmental hydrology and hydraulics, structural engineering, and 
transportation engineering) and three cross-cutting programs (sustainable and resilient 
infrastructure systems; energy, water, and environmental sustainability; and societal risk 
management). Although each area has its own special body of knowledge and engineering 
tools, they all rely on the same fundamental core principles. Civil engineering projects often 
draw on expertise from many of these areas and programs. 
Our Educa

l core principles. Civil engineering projects often 
draw on expertise from many of these areas and programs. 
Our Education Objectives are to educate Civil Engineering students to: 
1) 
Keep exploring new techniques and new knowledge for the sustainable development 
of human society.  
2) 
Successfully enter the civil and environmental engineering profession as practicing 
engineers and consultants with prominent companies and organizations in diverse areas that 
include structural, transportation, geotechnical, materials, environmental, and hydrologic 
engineering; construction management; or other related or emerging fields. 
3) 
Pursue graduate education and research at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) 
Pursue professional lic

at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) 
Pursue professional licensure and advance to leadership positions in the profession. 
5) 
Engage in continued learning through professional development. 
6) 
Participate in and contribute to professional societies and community services. 
During their junior and senior years, students are encouraged to take courses and involve 
themselves in research projects in cross-disciplinary areas with Electrical Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Computer Engineering, and others.

2. Graduation Requirement 
2.1 Graduation Requirements for UIUC degree 
2.1.1 
Grade Point Average Requirement 
A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and 
graduate. UIUC degree is subjected to the final review by UIUC.  
2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the CEE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years. 
2.1.3 
Curriculum Requirement 
The curriculum leading to the degree of Bachelor of Science in Civil Engineering at UIUC 
requires 128 academic credits

The curriculum leading to the degree of Bachelor of Science in Civil Engineering at UIUC 
requires 128 academic credits (or academic units, AUs) and is organized into required courses, 
technical electives, General education, and other electives.

i). Residency Requirement  
Earn a minimum sixty (60) academic units of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) 
AUs must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

4.2 Technical Electives 
This course work is designed to give each student a broad background in the areas of civil 
engineering through the core courses and to allow each student to develop a focused program 
through advanced technical electives in chosen primary and secondary fields. Briefly, there 
are two types of civil engineering courses:  
(1) core courses  
(2) advanced technical courses. 
The core courses are prerequisites for all advanced technical courses. The advanced technical 
courses are subdivided into a primary area of emphasis and a secondary area of emphasis. 
The core and secondary area courses assure adequate breadth in civil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth

ivil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth.  
Students cannot use an advanced technical course to meet both primary and secondary 
requirements.  
A student must have advanced technical electives as 12 AUs in the primary area and 6 AUs 
in the secondary area.

4.2.2 Advanced Technical Electives  
The advanced technical electives are selected to satisfy the requirements of a primary area of 
emphasis (i.e., a major field within civil engineering) and a secondary area of emphasis (i.e., 
a minor field within civil engineering). The program must have at least 12 AUs in the primary 
area and 6 AUs in the secondary area.  
ZJUI students are recommended to choose one as primary area from the following: 
(1) 
Water Resources Engineering & Science (WRES),  
(2) 
Structural Engineering (STRUCTURES),  
(3) 
Transportation Engineering (TRANSPORTATION). 
The courses in the secondary area of emphasis are chosen to complement the primary area 
and add brea

(TRANSPORTATION). 
The courses in the secondary area of emphasis are chosen to complement the primary area 
and add breadth to the program of study. Courses that make up a secondary area can be 
taken in one of the above three recommended areas of civil and environmental engineering, 
but there are also some additional options-CEE Multidisciplinary that give flexibility to the 
program.  
A secondary program CANNOT be taken in the same area as the primary. The secondary 
area requirement is meant to provide the student both with additional breadth and with an 
additional area of special focus.

I. 
Structural Engineering 
Structural engineering involves the analysis, design, and construction of buildings, dams, 
bridges, and other types of facilities. A structural engineer designs economical structures that 
satisfy requirements of safety, utility, and durability; oversees the building of constructed 
facilities; and investigates the performance of structures that fail to perform as expected. The 
tools of the structural engineer include physical testing, mathematical modeling, and computer 
simulation. The structural engineer uses these tools to make decisions that aid in the creation, 
maintenance, or demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiff

r demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiffel Tower, stand as monuments to the engineering 
achievements of humankind. The smallest of structures, such as thin films that contain 
computer circuitry, make possible many devices in our technologically sophisticated society.

II. 
Transportation Engineering 
Transportation engineers design, build, operate and maintain all types of facilities for railroads, 
automobiles, airplanes, and ships. They deal with the physical infrastructure of our 
transportation system, highways, ports, airports, and other facilities. They are involved in 
controlling traffic and in developing better transportation systems. Transportation engineers 
must understand the many factors that affect the long-term performance of the infrastructure 
from climatic factors imposed on it to the dynamics of the vehicles that use it. Transportation 
engineers are concerned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing socia

erned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing social, economic, geographical, and political needs of 
society.

III. 
Water Resources Engineering & Science 
Water Resources Engineering and Science deals with surface and ground water used for 
human consumption, energy, food, transportation, and recreation, as well as mitigation of 
floods and other water related hazards. This area comprises the fields of hydrology, hydraulics, 
and water resources planning. Hydrology is the study of water in the natural environment. 
Hydraulics studies how to design infrastructure to manage water flow and storage. Water 
resources planning concerns how to manage water efficiently and economically under 
hydrologic, hydraulic, economic, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and mana

c, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and management of facilities for domestic, 
municipal, industrial and agricultural water supply, drainage, control, and utilization. Such 
facilities include river channel-dam-reservoir systems for flood control, hydropower, navigation, 
water supply, and recreation; water distribution networks, sewer systems and culverts for 
urban water supply and drainage; injection/extraction well systems for remediation of 
contaminated ground water; and erosion and sediment control structures. Water Resources 
Engineers are also involved in the planning, operation, and management of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts a

agement of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts and climatic changes.

IV. 
The General Civil Engineering Option 
The General Option was created to recognize that there are career opportunities in Civil and 
Environmental Engineering for which great breadth of understanding of all aspects of Civil 
Engineering has merit over specialization. Some civil and environmental engineers solve 
problems, which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines. 
These problems can, and often do

which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines. 
These problems can, and often do, involve multiple teams with diverse expertise and 
background. Students choosing the General Civil Engineering option are encouraged to 
discuss with their faculty advisor and/or CEE advising staff.

4.4 Free Electives 
These unrestricted electives give students the opportunity to explore any intellectual area of 
unique interest. This freedom plays a critical role in helping students define their research 
specialties. Students are encouraged to take cross-disciplinary courses as free electives. 
Students must complete a minimum of six (6) AUs in free electives.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Civil engineering is a profession that applies the basic principles of science in conjunction with 
mathematical and computational tools to solve problems associated with developing and 
sustaining civilized life on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and the

ife on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and they usually require cooperation among 
professionals of many different disciplines. The completion of a civil engineering project 
involves the solution of technical problems in which uncertainty of information and myriad non-
technical factors often play a significant role. Some of the most common examples of civil 
engineering works include bridges, buildings, dams, airports, highways, tunnels, and water 
distribution systems. Civil engineers are concerned with flood control, landslides, air and water 
pollution, and the design of facilities to withstand earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil

earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil Engineering program comprises seven main areas (construction engineering and 
management, construction materials engineering, environmental engineering, geotechnical 
engineering, environmental hydrology and hydraulics, structural engineering, and 
transportation engineering) and three cross-cutting programs (sustainable and resilient 
infrastructure systems; energy, water, and environmental sustainability; and societal risk 
management). Although each area has its own special body of knowledge and engineering 
tools, they all rely on the same fundamental core principles. Civil engineering projects often 
draw on expertise from many of these areas and programs. 
Our Educa

l core principles. Civil engineering projects often 
draw on expertise from many of these areas and programs. 
Our Education Objectives are to educate Civil Engineering students to: 
1) 
Keep exploring new techniques and new knowledge for the sustainable development 
of human society.  
2) 
Successfully enter the civil and environmental engineering profession as practicing 
engineers and consultants with prominent companies and organizations in diverse areas that 
include structural, transportation, geotechnical, materials, environmental, and hydrologic 
engineering; construction management; or other related or emerging fields. 
3) 
Pursue graduate education and research at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) 
Pursue professional lic

at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) 
Pursue professional licensure and advance to leadership positions in the profession. 
5) 
Engage in continued learning through professional development. 
6) 
Participate in and contribute to professional societies and community services. 
During their junior and senior years, students are encouraged to take courses and involve 
themselves in research projects in cross-disciplinary areas with Electrical Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Computer Engineering, and others.

4.2 Technical Electives 
This course work is designed to give each student a broad background in the areas of civil 
engineering through the core courses and to allow each student to develop a focused program 
through advanced technical electives in chosen primary and secondary fields. Briefly, there 
are two types of civil engineering courses:  
(1) core courses  
(2) advanced technical courses. 
The core courses are prerequisites for all advanced technical courses. The advanced technical 
courses are subdivided into a primary area of emphasis and a secondary area of emphasis. 
The core and secondary area courses assure adequate breadth in civil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth

ivil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth.  
Students cannot use an advanced technical course to meet both primary and secondary 
requirements.  
A student must have advanced technical electives as 12 AUs in the primary area and 6 AUs 
in the secondary area.

I. 
Structural Engineering 
Structural engineering involves the analysis, design, and construction of buildings, dams, 
bridges, and other types of facilities. A structural engineer designs economical structures that 
satisfy requirements of safety, utility, and durability; oversees the building of constructed 
facilities; and investigates the performance of structures that fail to perform as expected. The 
tools of the structural engineer include physical testing, mathematical modeling, and computer 
simulation. The structural engineer uses these tools to make decisions that aid in the creation, 
maintenance, or demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiff

r demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiffel Tower, stand as monuments to the engineering 
achievements of humankind. The smallest of structures, such as thin films that contain 
computer circuitry, make possible many devices in our technologically sophisticated society.

II. 
Transportation Engineering 
Transportation engineers design, build, operate and maintain all types of facilities for railroads, 
automobiles, airplanes, and ships. They deal with the physical infrastructure of our 
transportation system, highways, ports, airports, and other facilities. They are involved in 
controlling traffic and in developing better transportation systems. Transportation engineers 
must understand the many factors that affect the long-term performance of the infrastructure 
from climatic factors imposed on it to the dynamics of the vehicles that use it. Transportation 
engineers are concerned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing socia

erned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing social, economic, geographical, and political needs of 
society.

III. 
Water Resources Engineering & Science 
Water Resources Engineering and Science deals with surface and ground water used for 
human consumption, energy, food, transportation, and recreation, as well as mitigation of 
floods and other water related hazards. This area comprises the fields of hydrology, hydraulics, 
and water resources planning. Hydrology is the study of water in the natural environment. 
Hydraulics studies how to design infrastructure to manage water flow and storage. Water 
resources planning concerns how to manage water efficiently and economically under 
hydrologic, hydraulic, economic, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and mana

c, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and management of facilities for domestic, 
municipal, industrial and agricultural water supply, drainage, control, and utilization. Such 
facilities include river channel-dam-reservoir systems for flood control, hydropower, navigation, 
water supply, and recreation; water distribution networks, sewer systems and culverts for 
urban water supply and drainage; injection/extraction well systems for remediation of 
contaminated ground water; and erosion and sediment control structures. Water Resources 
Engineers are also involved in the planning, operation, and management of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts a

agement of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts and climatic changes.

The General Option was created to recognize that there are career opportunities in Civil and 
Environmental Engineering for which great breadth of understanding of all aspects of Civil 
Engineering has merit over specialization. Some civil and environmental engineers solve 
problems, which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines. 
These problems can, and often do

which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines. 
These problems can, and often do, involve multiple teams with diverse expertise and 
background. Students choosing the General Civil Engineering option are encouraged to 
discuss with their faculty advisor and/or CEE advising staff.

4.4 Free Electives 
These unrestricted electives give students the opportunity to explore any intellectual area of 
unique interest. This freedom plays a critical role in helping students define their research 
specialties. Students are encouraged to take cross-disciplinary courses as free electives. 
Students must complete a minimum of six (6) AUs in free electives.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Civil engineering is a profession that applies the basic principles of science in conjunction with 
mathematical and computational tools to solve problems associated with developing and 
sustaining civilized life on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and the

ife on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and they usually require cooperation among 
professionals of many different disciplines. The completion of a civil engineering project 
involves the solution of technical problems in which uncertainty of information and myriad non-
technical factors often play a significant role. Some of the most common examples of civil 
engineering works include bridges, buildings, dams, airports, highways, tunnels, and water 
distribution systems. Civil engineers are concerned with flood control, landslides, air and water 
pollution, and the design of facilities to withstand earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil

earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil Engineering program comprises seven main areas (construction engineering and 
management, construction materials engineering, environmental engineering, geotechnical 
engineering, environmental hydrology and hydraulics, structural engineering, and 
transportation engineering) and three cross-cutting programs (sustainable and resilient 
infrastructure systems; energy, water, and environmental sustainability; and societal risk 
management). Although each area has its own special body of knowledge and engineering 
tools, they all rely on the same fundamental core principles. Civil engineering projects often 
draw on expertise from many of these areas and programs. 
Our Educa

l core principles. Civil engineering projects often 
draw on expertise from many of these areas and programs. 
Our Education Objectives are to educate Civil Engineering students to: 
1) 
Keep exploring new techniques and new knowledge for the sustainable development 
of human society.  
2) 
Successfully enter the civil and environmental engineering profession as practicing 
engineers and consultants with prominent companies and organizations in diverse areas that 
include structural, transportation, geotechnical, materials, environmental, and hydrologic 
engineering; construction management; or other related or emerging fields. 
3) 
Pursue graduate education and research at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) 
Pursue professional lic

at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) 
Pursue professional licensure and advance to leadership positions in the profession. 
5) 
Engage in continued learning through professional development. 
6) 
Participate in and contribute to professional societies and community services. 
During their junior and senior years, students are encouraged to take courses and involve 
themselves in research projects in cross-disciplinary areas with Electrical Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Computer Engineering, and others.

4.2 Technical Electives 
This course work is designed to give each student a broad background in the areas of civil 
engineering through the core courses and to allow each student to develop a focused program 
through advanced technical electives in chosen primary and secondary fields. Briefly, there 
are two types of civil engineering courses:  
(1) core courses  
(2) advanced technical courses. 
The core courses are prerequisites for all advanced technical courses. The advanced technical 
courses are subdivided into a primary area of emphasis and a secondary area of emphasis. 
The core and secondary area courses assure adequate breadth in civil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth

ivil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth.  
Students cannot use an advanced technical course to meet both primary and secondary 
requirements.  
A student must have advanced technical electives as 12 AUs in the primary area and 6 AUs 
in the secondary area.

The advanced technical electives are selected to satisfy the requirements of a primary area of

area and 6 AUs in the secondary area.

Science (WRES).

I. 
Structural Engineering 
Structural engineering involves the analysis, design, and construction of buildings, dams, 
bridges, and other types of facilities. A structural engineer designs economical structures that 
satisfy requirements of safety, utility, and durability; oversees the building of constructed 
facilities; and investigates the performance of structures that fail to perform as expected. The 
tools of the structural engineer include physical testing, mathematical modeling, and computer 
simulation. The structural engineer uses these tools to make decisions that aid in the creation, 
maintenance, or demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiff

r demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiffel Tower, stand as monuments to the engineering 
achievements of humankind. The smallest of structures, such as thin films that contain 
computer circuitry, make possible many devices in our technologically sophisticated society.

II. 
Transportation Engineering 
Transportation engineers design, build, operate and maintain all types of facilities for railroads, 
automobiles, airplanes, and ships. They deal with the physical infrastructure of our 
transportation system, highways, ports, airports, and other facilities. They are involved in 
controlling traffic and in developing better transportation systems. Transportation engineers 
must understand the many factors that affect the long-term performance of the infrastructure 
from climatic factors imposed on it to the dynamics of the vehicles that use it. Transportation 
engineers are concerned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing socia

erned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing social, economic, geographical, and political needs of 
society.

III. 
Water Resources Engineering & Science 
Water Resources Engineering and Science deals with surface and ground water used for 
human consumption, energy, food, transportation, and recreation, as well as mitigation of 
floods and other water related hazards. This area comprises the fields of hydrology, hydraulics, 
and water resources planning. Hydrology is the study of water in the natural environment. 
Hydraulics studies how to design infrastructure to manage water flow and storage. Water 
resources planning concerns how to manage water efficiently and economically under 
hydrologic, hydraulic, economic, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and mana

c, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and management of facilities for domestic, 
municipal, industrial and agricultural water supply, drainage, control, and utilization. Such 
facilities include river channel-dam-reservoir systems for flood control, hydropower, navigation, 
water supply, and recreation; water distribution networks, sewer systems and culverts for 
urban water supply and drainage; injection/extraction well systems for remediation of 
contaminated ground water; and erosion and sediment control structures. Water Resources 
Engineers are also involved in the planning, operation, and management of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts a

agement of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts and climatic changes.

Environmental Engineering for which great breadth of understanding of all aspects of Civil 
Engineering has merit over specialization. Some civil and environmental engineers solve 
problems, which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines.

which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines.

For courses that fail in multiple categories of general education, the academic units of the 
course will be counted to satisfy the AU requirements of each category but be counted only 
once in satisfying the total minimum academic unit requirements (12 AUs) of general education.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Civil engineering is a profession that applies the basic principles of science in conjunction with 
mathematical and computational tools to solve problems associated with developing and 
sustaining civilized life on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and the

ife on our planet. Civil engineering works are generally one-of-a-kind 
projects; they are often grand in scale; and they usually require cooperation among 
professionals of many different disciplines. The completion of a civil engineering project 
involves the solution of technical problems in which uncertainty of information and myriad non-
technical factors often play a significant role. Some of the most common examples of civil 
engineering works include bridges, buildings, dams, airports, highways, tunnels, and water 
distribution systems. Civil engineers are concerned with flood control, landslides, air and water 
pollution, and the design of facilities to withstand earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil

earthquakes and other natural hazards, in 
addition to protecting our environment for a sustainable future. 
The Civil Engineering program comprises three areas (environmental hydrology and 
hydraulics, structural engineering, and transportation engineering). Although each area has its 
own special body of knowledge and engineering tools, they all rely on the same fundamental 
core principles. Civil engineering projects often draw on expertise from many of these areas 
and programs. 
Our Education Objectives are to educate Civil Engineering students to: 
1) Keep exploring new techniques and new knowledge for the sustainable development of 
human society.  
2) Successfully enter the civil and environmental engineering profession as practicing 
engineers and consultants with prominent compa

y enter the civil and environmental engineering profession as practicing 
engineers and consultants with prominent companies and organizations in diverse areas 
that include structural, transportation, geotechnical, materials, environmental, and 
hydrologic engineering; construction management; or other related or emerging fields. 
3) Pursue graduate education and research at major research universities in civil and 
environmental engineering, and related fields. 
4) Pursue professional licensure and advance to leadership positions in the profession. 
5) Engage in continued learning through professional development. 
6) Participate in and contribute to professional societies and community services. 
During their junior and senior years, students are encouraged to take courses and involve

ies and community services. 
During their junior and senior years, students are encouraged to take courses and involve 
themselves in research projects in cross-disciplinary areas with Electrical Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Computer Engineering, and others.

2. Graduation Requirement 
2.1 Graduation Requirements for UIUC degree 
2.1.1 
Grade Point Average Requirement 
A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and 
graduate. UIUC degree is subjected to the final review by UIUC. 
2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the CEE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and civil engineering courses listed in the first two years.

i). Residency Requirement  
Earn a minimum sixty (60) academic credits of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) 
AUs must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

4.2 Technical Electives 
This course work is designed to give each student a broad background in the areas of civil 
engineering thr

l Electives 
This course work is designed to give each student a broad background in the areas of civil 
engineering through the core courses and to allow each student to develop a focused program 
through advanced technical electives in chosen primary and secondary fields. Briefly, there 
are two types of technical elective courses in civil engineering:  
(1) core courses  
(2) advanced technical courses. 
The core courses are prerequisites for all advanced technical courses. The advanced technical 
courses are subdivided into a primary area of emphasis and a secondary area of emphasis. 
The core and secondary area courses assure adequate breadth in civil engineering subjects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth. 
Students cannot u

jects, 
while the primary area courses allow the student to study a certain subject in greater depth. 
Students cannot use an advanced technical course to meet both primary and secondary 
requirements. A student must have advanced technical electives as 12 AUs in the primary 
area and 6 AUs in the secondary area. The sum of academic credit hours of core courses and 
technical electives must be at least thirty-four (34).

4.2.2 Advanced Technical Electives  
The advanced technical electives are selected to satisfy the requirements of a prim

4.2.2 Advanced Technical Electives  
The advanced technical electives are selected to satisfy the requirements of a primary area of 
emphasis (i.e., a major field within civil engineering) and a secondary area of emphasis (i.e., 
a minor field within civil engineering). The program must have at least 12 AUs in the primary 
area and 6 AUs in the secondary area.  
ZJUI students are recommended to choose one as primary area from the following: 
(1) 
Structural Engineering (STRUCTURES), 
(2) 
Transportation Engineering (TRANSPORTATION), 
(3) 
Water Resources Engineering & Science (WRES). 
The courses in the secondary area of emphasis are chosen to complement the primary area 
and add breadth to the program of study. Courses that make up a secondary area can be 
taken in one of the above three

and add breadth to the program of study. Courses that make up a secondary area can be 
taken in one of the above three recommended areas of civil and environmental engineering, 
but there are also some additional options-CEE Multidisciplinary that give flexibility to the 
program.  
A secondary program CANNOT be taken in the same area as the primary. The secondary 
area requirement is meant to provide the student both with additional breadth and with an 
additional area of special focus. 
Students who are pursuing only ZJU degree and take only four (4) core courses, need to take 
at least 7 advanced technical electives and obtain no less than thirty-four (34) credits in 
technical electives.

I. Structural Engineering 
Structural engineering involves the analysis, design, and construction of buildings, dams, 
bridges, and other types of facilities. A structural engineer designs economical structures that 
satisfy requirements of safety, utility, and durability; oversees the building of constructed 
facilities; and investigates the performance of structures that fail to perform as expected. The 
tools of the structural engineer include physical testing, mathematical modeling, and computer 
simulation. The structural engineer uses these tools to make decisions that aid in the creation, 
maintenance, or demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macao Bridge, the Golden

demolition of constructed facilities. The largest of structures, such as the 
Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macao Bridge, the Golden Gate Bridge, Hoover Dam, and Eiffel Tower, 
stand as monuments to the engineering achievements of humankind. The smallest of 
structures, such as thin films that contain computer circuitry, make possible many devices in 
our technologically sophisticated society.

II. 
Transportation Engineering 
Transportation engineers design, build, operate and maintain all types of facilities for railroads, 
automobiles, airplanes, and ships. They deal with the physical infrastructure of our 
transportation system, highways, ports, airports, and other facilities. They are involved in 
controlling traffic and in developing better transportation systems. Transportation engineers 
must understand the many factors that affect the long-term performance of the infrastructure 
from climatic factors imposed on it to the dynamics of the vehicles that use it. Transportation 
engineers are concerned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing soc

ncerned with the development and operation of our multi-modal transportation 
system to meet the constantly changing social, economic, geographical, and political needs of 
society.

III. 
Water Resources Engineering & Science 
Water Resources Engineering and Science deals with surface and ground water used for 
human consumption, energy, food, transportation, and recreation, as well as mitigation of 
floods and other water related hazards. This area comprises the fields of hydrology, hydraulics, 
and water resources planning. Hydrology is the study of water in the natural environment. 
Hydraulics studies how to design infrastructure to manage water flow and storage. Water 
resources planning concerns how to manage water efficiently and economically under 
hydrologic, hydraulic, economic, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline ar

ently and economically under 
hydrologic, hydraulic, economic, and societal constraints. Engineers in this discipline are 
responsible for the planning, design, operation, and management of facilities for domestic, 
municipal, industrial and agricultural water supply, drainage, control, and utilization. Such 
facilities include river channel-dam-reservoir systems for flood control, hydropower, navigation, 
water supply, and recreation; water distribution networks, sewer systems and culverts for 
urban water supply and drainage; injection/extraction well systems for remediation of 
contaminated ground water; and erosion and sediment control structures. Water Resources 
Engineers are also involved in the planning, operation, and management of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well

ineers are also involved in the planning, operation, and management of ground water, 
watersheds, and wetlands, as well as the hydrologic environment's response to human 
impacts and climatic changes.

Environmental Engineering for which great breadth of understanding of all aspects of Civil 
Engineering has merit over specialization. Some civil and environmental engineers solve 
problems, which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines. 
These problems can, and often do

which require the interaction with other engineering and non-engineering disciplines. 
These problems can, and often do, involve multiple teams with diverse expertise and 
background. Students choosing the General Civil Engineering option are encouraged to 
discuss with their faculty advisor and/or CEE advising staff.

4.4 Free Electives 
These unrestricted electives give students the opportunity to explore any intellectual area of 
unique interest. This freedom plays a critical role in helping students define their research 
specialties. Students are encouraged to take cross-disciplinary courses as free electives. 
Students must complete a minimum of six (6) AUs in free electives. 
ZJU required general education courses cannot be counted as free electives to satisfy with 
the requirements of UIUC degree.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and University of Illinois Urbana-
Champaign (UIUC). 
Computer engineering focuses on the development of vital computing technologies, ranging 
from chips to computers to networks to programming tools to key algorithms for building 
exciting applications. Fundamentally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the faculty'

tally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the faculty's interests span a broad spectrum of 
issues pertinent to this theme. Computer engineering has taken the lead in revolutionizing 
many science and engineering disciplines with parallel computing, from chips to clouds to 
planet-scale critical infrastructures, and has defined new standards of security, privacy, and 
dependability for systems ranging from small circuits to the electric power grids of many 
nations. Students need a broad and sound set of mathematical and computing skills and are 
well-served by a flexible curriculum that enables them to pursue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineering pr

sue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineering program focuses on the fundamental 
computer engineering knowledge: circuits, systems, electromagnetics, computer systems, 
electronics for information processing and communication, and computer science. The rich set 
of ECE elective courses permits students to concentrate on any sub-discipline of computer 
engineering, including hardware systems; cyber physical systems; foundations and theory; 
software and languages; algorithms and mathematical tools; trust, reliability, security; 
networking, mobile and distributed computing; big data analytics and systems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encouraged to t

ems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encouraged to take courses and participate 
in research projects in interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Electrical Engineering, and others.

2.1.1 
Grade Point Average Requirement 
A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and 
graduate. UIUC degree is subjected to the final review by UIUC. 
2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the ECE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
compute

curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years.

2.1.4 
Additional requirements  
For UIUC degree, in addition to the specific course and grade point average requirements 
listed above, each candidate for a bachelor’s degree from UIUC must meet the following 
requirements: 
i). Residency Requirement  
Earn a minimum sixty (60) academic units of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) 
AUs must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that

ency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and University of Illinois Urbana-
Champaign (UIUC). 
Computer engineering focuses on the development of vital computing technologies, ranging 
from chips to computers to networks to programming tools to key algorithms for building 
exciting applications. Fundamentally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the faculty'

tally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the faculty's interests span a broad spectrum of 
issues pertinent to this theme. Computer engineering has taken the lead in revolutionizing 
many science and engineering disciplines with parallel computing, from chips to clouds to 
planet-scale critical infrastructures, and has defined new standards of security, privacy, and 
dependability for systems ranging from small circuits to the electric power grids of many 
nations. Students need a broad and sound set of mathematical and computing skills and are 
well-served by a flexible curriculum that enables them to pursue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineering pr

sue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineering program focuses on the fundamental 
computer engineering knowledge: circuits, systems, electromagnetics, computer systems, 
electronics for information processing and communication, and computer science. The rich set 
of ECE elective courses permits students to concentrate on any sub-discipline of computer 
engineering, including hardware systems; cyber physical systems; foundations and theory; 
software and languages; algorithms and mathematical tools; trust, reliability, security; 
networking, mobile and distributed computing; big data analytics and systems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encouraged to t

ems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encouraged to take courses and participate 
in research projects in interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Electrical Engineering, and others. 
 
2. Graduation Requirement 
2.1 Graduation Requirements for UIUC degree 
2.1.1 
Grade Point Average Requirement 
A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and 
graduate. UIUC degree is subjected to the final review by UIUC. 
2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the ECE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
compute

curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years.

2.1.4 
Additional requirements  
For UIUC degree, in addition to the specific course and grade point average requirements 
listed above, each candidate for a bachelor’s degree from UIUC must meet the following 
requirements: 
i). Residency Requirement  
Earn a minimum sixty (60) academic units of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) 
AUs must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies t

ficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute, 
ZJUI) offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the 
program, and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will 
obtain bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Computer engineering focuses on the development of vital computing technologies, ranging 
from chips to computers to networks to programming tools to key algorithms for building 
exciting applications. Fundamentally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the fa

ndamentally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the faculty's interests span a broad spectrum of 
issues pertinent to this theme. Computer engineering has taken the lead in revolutionizing 
many science and engineering disciplines with parallel computing, from chips to clouds to 
planet-scale critical infrastructures, and has defined new standards of security, privacy, and 
dependability for systems ranging from small circuits to the electric power grids of many 
nations. Students need a broad and sound set of mathematical and computing skills and are 
well-served by a flexible curriculum that enables them to pursue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineer

to pursue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineering program focuses on the fundamental 
computer engineering knowledge: circuits, systems, electromagnetics, computer systems, 
electronics for information processing and communication, and computer science. The rich set 
of ECE elective courses permits students to concentrate on any sub-discipline of computer 
engineering, including hardware systems; cyber physical systems; foundations and theory; 
software and languages; algorithms and mathematical tools; trust, reliability, security; 
networking, mobile and distributed computing; big data analytics and systems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encourage

d systems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encouraged to take courses and participate 
in research projects in interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Electrical Engineering, and others.

2.1 Graduation Requirements for UIUC degree 
2.1.1 
Grade Point Average Requirement 
A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and 
graduate. UIUC degree is subjected to the final review by UIUC. 
2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the ECE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics,

must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years.

technical electives, general education, and other electives.

2.1.4 
Additional requirements  
For UIUC degree, in addition to the specific course and grade point average requirements 
listed above, each candidate for a bachelor’s degree from UIUC must meet the following 
requirements: 
i). Residency Requirement  
Earn a minimum sixty (60) academic credits of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) 
AUs must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing o

sfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute, 
ZJUI) offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the 
program, and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will 
obtain bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Computer engineering focuses on the development of vital computing technologies, ranging 
from chips to computers to networks to programming tools to key algorithms for building 
exciting applications. Fundamentally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the fa

ndamentally, computer engineering addresses the problem of building 
scalable, trustworthy computing systems, and the faculty's interests span a broad spectrum of 
issues pertinent to this theme. Computer engineering has taken the lead in revolutionizing 
many science and engineering disciplines with parallel computing, from chips to clouds to 
planet-scale critical infrastructures, and has defined new standards of security, privacy, and 
dependability for systems ranging from small circuits to the electric power grids of many 
nations. Students need a broad and sound set of mathematical and computing skills and are 
well-served by a flexible curriculum that enables them to pursue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineer

to pursue topics of interest among the 
many sub-disciplines in computing. 
The core curriculum of the Computer Engineering program focuses on the fundamental 
computer engineering knowledge: circuits, systems, electromagnetics, computer systems, 
electronics for information processing and communication, and computer science. The rich set 
of ECE elective courses permits students to concentrate on any sub-discipline of computer 
engineering, including hardware systems; cyber physical systems; foundations and theory; 
software and languages; algorithms and mathematical tools; trust, reliability, security; 
networking, mobile and distributed computing; big data analytics and systems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encourage

d systems; artificial 
intelligence, robotics, cybernetics. 
During their junior and senior years students are encouraged to take courses and participate 
in research projects in interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, 
Mechanical Engineering, Electrical Engineering, and others. 
 
2. Graduation Requirement 
2.1 Graduation Requirements for UIUC degree 
2.1.1 
Grade Point Average Requirement 
A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and 
graduate. UIUC degree is subjected to the final review by UIUC. 
2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the ECE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics,

must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years.

technical electives, general education, and other electives.

2.1.4 
Additional requirements  
For UIUC degree, in addition to the specific course and grade point average requirements 
listed above, each candidate for a bachelor’s degree from UIUC must meet the following 
requirements: 
i). Residency Requirement  
Earn a minimum sixty (60) academic units of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) 
AUs must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on

actory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall Technical GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, 
students will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic 
status from non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

systems; foundations and theory; software and languages; algorithms and mathematical tools; 
trust, reliability, security; networking, mobile and distributed computing; big data analytics and 
systems; artificial intelligence, robotics, cybernetics, etc.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Electrical engineering is a multifaceted discipline that over the last century has produced an 
astounding progression of technological innovations that have shaped virtually every aspect 
of modern life. Electrical engineers need a broad and solid foundation in mathematics and 
physics to support their education in the en

Electrical engineers need a broad and solid foundation in mathematics and 
physics to support their education in the engineering principles of analysis, synthesis, design, 
implementation, and testing of the devices and systems that provide the bedrock of modern 
energy, communication, sensing, computing, medical, security, and defense infrastructures. 
Within each sub-discipline, there are application domains that strongly rely on hands-on 
experimental work or that are based on theoretical, mathematical, and computational 
approaches. The multidisciplinary nature of electrical engineering education addresses the 
growing demand for the innovation and design of sensing, communication, computing, and 
decision-making systems of increasing complexity in consumer, defense, and medical 
appl

communication, computing, and 
decision-making systems of increasing complexity in consumer, defense, and medical 
applications. 
The core curriculum focuses on fundamental courses on circuits, electromagnetics, solid-state 
electronics, and computer systems, leading to a comprehensive array of specialized courses 
and laboratories in all of the important areas of modern electrical engineering. These range 
from power and energy systems to electronic, optoelectronic, and photonic devices; integrated 
circuits; telecommunications and remote sensing; control systems; robotics; signal processing; 
and biomedical instrumentation and sensing. 
Students are encouraged to take courses and participate in research projects in 
interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, Mecha

courses and participate in research projects in 
interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, 
Computer Engineering, and others during their junior and senior years.

A student must maintain a minimum GPA of 2.0 (A=4.0) to remain in good standing and

2.1.2 
Junior Eligibility Requirement 
To be eligible to enroll in the ECE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years.

Additional requirements  
For UIUC degree, in addition to the specific course and grade point average requirements

requirements: 
i). Residency Requirement

Earn a minimum sixty (60) semester AUs of UIUC credit, of which at least twenty-one (21) AUs 
must be 300 or 400 level UIUC credit courses.  
ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

Bachelor of Engineering from ZJU requires students to complete additional 33.5 credits of 
ZJU-required general education courses for domestic students and additional six courses (22 
credits) in Chinese language and society study for international students. Please refer to 
section 5 for details.

4. Elective Courses 
4.1 Technical Electives 
This elective requirement gives each student the freedom to develop a t

4. Elective Courses 
4.1 Technical Electives 
This elective requirement gives each student the freedom to develop a technical course of 
study in electrical engineering of considerable breadth and focus. The Advanced Core ECE 
Electives provide an introduction to the major sub-disciplines of electrical engineering, such 
as electrical and computer engineering; bioengineering; acoustics; and magnetic resonance 
engineering; circuits and signal processing; communication and control; computer engineering; 
electromagnetics; optics; and remote sensing; microelectronics and quantum electronics; 
power and energy systems.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute) 
offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the program, 
and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will obtain 
bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Electrical engineering is a multifaceted discipline that over the last century has produced an 
astounding progression of technological innovations that have shaped virtually every aspect 
of modern life. Electrical engineers need a broad and solid foundation in mathematics and 
physics to support their education in the en

Electrical engineers need a broad and solid foundation in mathematics and 
physics to support their education in the engineering principles of analysis, synthesis, design, 
implementation, and testing of the devices and systems that provide the bedrock of modern 
energy, communication, sensing, computing, medical, security, and defense infrastructures. 
Within each sub-discipline, there are application domains that strongly rely on hands-on 
experimental work or that are based on theoretical, mathematical, and computational 
approaches. The multidisciplinary nature of electrical engineering education addresses the 
growing demand for the innovation and design of sensing, communication, computing, and 
decision-making systems of increasing complexity in consumer, defense, and medical 
appl

communication, computing, and 
decision-making systems of increasing complexity in consumer, defense, and medical 
applications. 
The core curriculum focuses on fundamental courses on circuits, electromagnetics, solid-state 
electronics, and computer systems, leading to a comprehensive array of specialized courses 
and laboratories in all of the important areas of modern electrical engineering. These range 
from power and energy systems to electronic, optoelectronic, and photonic devices; integrated 
circuits; telecommunications and remote sensing; control systems; robotics; signal processing; 
and biomedical instrumentation and sensing. 
Students are encouraged to take courses and participate in research projects in 
interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, Mecha

courses and participate in research projects in 
interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, 
Computer Engineering, and others during their junior and senior years.

To be eligible to enroll in the ECE courses listed in the third year of the curriculum, a student 
must have completed, with a combined 2.25 grade point average, the mathematics, physics, 
computer science, and electrical and computer engineering courses listed in the first two years.

ii). Transfer Requirement  
Students should have a satisfactory English Proficiency Test score on TOEFL, IELTS or other 
exams approved by UIUC, and maintain a good standing on academic studies that all term 
GPAs, overall GPA on UIUC courses are suggested to be above 2.5. In junior year, students 
will apply for international transfer to be admitted to UIUC and change academic status from 
non-degree student to degree student in Fall semester of senior year.

4. Elective Courses 
4.1 Technical Electives 
This elective requirement gives each student the freedom to develop a technical course of 
study in electrical engineering of considerable breadth and focus. The Advanced Core ECE 
Electives provide an introduction to the major sub-disciplines of electrical engineering, such 
as electrical and computer engineering; bioengineering; acoustics; and magnetic resonance 
engineering; circuits and signal processing; communication and control; computer engineering; 
electromagnetics; optics; and remote sensing; microelectronics and quantum electronics; 
power and energy systems.

1. Overview 
The Zhejiang University/University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Institute (ZJU-UIUC Institute, 
ZJUI) offers a joint dual-degree engineering program. Upon successful completion of the 
program, and after meeting the graduation requirements of both universities, students will 
obtain bachelor’s degrees separately from Zhejiang University (ZJU) and the University of 
Illinois Urbana-Champaign (UIUC). 
Electrical engineering is a multifaceted discipline that over the last century has produced an 
astounding progression of technological innovations that have shaped virtually every aspect 
of modern life. Electrical engineers need a broad and solid foundation in mathematics and 
physics to support their education in

life. Electrical engineers need a broad and solid foundation in mathematics and 
physics to support their education in the engineering principles of analysis, synthesis, design, 
implementation, and testing of the devices and systems that provide the bedrock of modern 
energy, communication, sensing, computing, medical, security, and defense infrastructures. 
Within each sub-discipline, there are application domains that strongly rely on hands-on 
experimental work or that are based on theoretical, mathematical, and computational 
approaches. The multidisciplinary nature of electrical engineering education addresses the 
growing demand for the innovation and design of sensing, communication, computing, and 
decision-making systems of increasing complexity in consumer, defense, and medical

nsing, communication, computing, and 
decision-making systems of increasing complexity in consumer, defense, and medical 
applications. 
The core curriculum focuses on fundamental courses on circuits, electromagnetics, solid-state 
electronics, and computer systems, leading to a comprehensive array of specialized courses 
and laboratories in all of the important areas of modern electrical engineering. These range 
from power and energy systems to electronic, optoelectronic, and photonic devices; integrated 
circuits; telecommunications and remote sensing; control systems; robotics; signal processing; 
and biomedical instrumentation and sensing. 
Students are encouraged to take courses and participate in research projects in 
interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering,

take courses and participate in research projects in 
interdisciplinary areas with Civil and Environmental Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, 
Computer Engineering, and others during their junior and senior years.
